core_meaning: To look at, watch, or see something — but also carries the sense of deliberately directing your attention toward something. Unlike English 'see' (which can be passive), <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る almost always implies some degree of intentional looking. It also appears constantly as a grammar helper (~てみる) meaning 'try doing something.'

sentences.jp: ねえ、これ<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>て！すごくない？
sentences.translation: Hey, look at this! Isn't it amazing?
sentences.notes: The bare て-form <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>て as a command is the most natural casual way to get someone to look at something — not <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>てください, which would sound stiff between friends.

sentences.jp: <ruby>昨日<rt>きのう</rt></ruby>、<ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>になってた<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>やっと<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>た。めっちゃよかった。
sentences.translation: I finally watched that movie I'd been curious about yesterday. It was so good.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is the standard verb for watching films, TV, and video — not <ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>く or <ruby>読<rt>よ</rt></ruby>む.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>薬<rt>くすり</rt></ruby>が<ruby>効<rt>き</rt></ruby>くかどうか、まず<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んでみてください。
sentences.translation: Please try taking this medicine first and see if it works.
sentences.notes: ~てみる means 'try doing X to see what happens' — one of the most important grammatical uses of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る, and it attaches to the て-form of other verbs.

sentences.jp: <ruby>部長<rt>ぶちょう</rt></ruby>、この<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>をご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>になりましたか？
sentences.translation: Section chief, have you looked at these documents?
sentences.notes: In formal/keigo contexts, <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る becomes ご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>になる (respectful) or <ruby>拝見<rt>はいけん</rt></ruby>する (humble) — using plain <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る with superiors sounds rude.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る、という<ruby>言葉<rt>ことば</rt></ruby>には、ただ<ruby>目<rt>め</rt></ruby>で<ruby>追<rt>お</rt></ruby>うだけでなく、<ruby>世話<rt>せわ</rt></ruby>をするという<ruby>意味<rt>いみ</rt></ruby>も<ruby>含<rt>ふく</rt></ruby>まれている。
sentences.translation: The phrase 'to see to a child' carries not just the meaning of watching with your eyes, but also taking care of them.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る can mean 'to look after / tend to' someone or something — a broader sense that goes beyond visual perception.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when something entered your field of vision without intention — use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える for 'can be seen' or 'is visible' instead (e.g. <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える, not <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る, for 'the mountain can be seen'). Also avoid plain <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when speaking to or about superiors — swap in ご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>になる or <ruby>拝見<rt>はいけん</rt></ruby>する. Don't confuse <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る with <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>つける ('to find/spot something'), which implies discovery.

confused_with.contrast: A common learner mistake is saying 「あそこに<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る」when they mean the mountain is visible in the distance — but this sounds like you're actively staring at it on purpose. The natural form is 「あそこに<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える」, which means it can be seen / is visible without implying effort. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when you choose to look; use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える when something is simply visible or perceivable.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is one of the most common verbs in Japanese, appearing constantly in daily speech, writing, and media. Its grammatical helper form ~てみる is equally ubiquitous. There are no register restrictions on the plain form itself, though keigo substitutes are required in formal hierarchical situations.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る = intentional looking (you choose to look); <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える = something is just visible — and remember that ~てみる ('try doing') is <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る hiding as grammar.
